title: BFS: Level Order Traversal
description: Visiting a tree level by level using a Queue, instead of diving deep like the three Tree Traversals -- Breadth-First Search applied to trees.
objectives: Understand level order traversal as Breadth-First Search on a tree
Group results by level, a common variant of the base pattern

sections.text: Pre-order, in-order, and post-order (Tree Traversals) all dive DEEP down one branch before backtracking -- Depth-First. Level order traversal instead visits every node on one LEVEL before moving to the next -- Breadth-First Search (BFS), the tree version of the same idea from graphs (covered fully in the Graphs module).
Visiting a tree's nodes level by level, from top to bottom and left to right within each level, typically implemented using a Queue.

Recall from Queue and Deque: a Queue is FIFO (first-in-first-out). Level order traversal needs exactly this -- process nodes in the order they were DISCOVERED, level by level, which a Queue naturally provides. This is why level order is usually written iteratively with a Queue, unlike the three recursive depth-first traversals.

sections.title: Tracing levelOrder on the tree 1(2(4,5),3)
steps: queue=[1]. Poll 1, print 1. Offer children 2,3. queue=[2,3]

sections.text: Note the OUTPUT ORDER differs from all three depth-first traversals from Tree Traversals -- 1 2 3 4 5 here, versus 1 2 4 5 3 (pre-order), 4 2 5 1 3 (in-order), or 4 5 2 3 1 (post-order) for this exact same tree. Level order visits by DEPTH first, then left-to-right; depth-first traversals dive down one branch entirely before backtracking.

int levelSize = queue.size(); MUST be captured BEFORE the for loop starts polling -- children get offered into the SAME queue during that loop, growing its size. Without snapshotting levelSize first, the loop would incorrectly also consume the next level's nodes in the same iteration.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using recursion for level order traversal by habit -- it's naturally iterative, built around a Queue's FIFO behavior, unlike the three depth-first traversals.
Forgetting to snapshot queue.size() before the inner loop when grouping by level, causing later levels to bleed into the current one.
Confusing level order's output with any of the three depth-first traversal orders -- they are genuinely different traversal STRATEGIES, not just different code for the same result.
Forgetting the root == null check, which would otherwise attempt queue.offer(null) and process a null node.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is level order traversal, and how does it differ from pre/in/post-order?
questions.answer: Level order visits nodes level by level, top to bottom and left to right, using Breadth-First Search. Pre/in/post-order are all Depth-First, diving fully down one branch before backtracking -- they produce a different visiting order entirely, not just a different implementation of the same order.

questions.question: Why is a Queue used for level order traversal instead of recursion?
questions.answer: A Queue's FIFO behavior naturally processes nodes in the order they were discovered, which is exactly what visiting level by level requires -- recursion's call stack instead naturally supports depth-first, LIFO-like exploration.

questions.question: When grouping level order results by level, why must queue.size() be captured before the inner loop begins?
questions.answer: Because children get added to the same queue during the inner loop, growing its size -- capturing levelSize beforehand ensures the loop processes exactly the current level's nodes, not any of the next level's nodes that get added partway through.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Level order traversal (BFS on a tree) visits nodes level by level, top to bottom and left to right, contrasting with pre/in/post-order's depth-first diving down one branch at a time. It's implemented using a Queue: offer the root, then repeatedly poll a node, print it, and offer its non-null children, until the queue is empty. Grouping results by level requires snapshotting queue.size() before each level's inner loop begins, since children get added to the same queue during that loop and would otherwise bleed into the current level's processing.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Level order = reading a family tree generation by generation, left to right, never skipping ahead to grandchildren before finishing the children. Queue in, queue out -- FIFO is the whole trick.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: How does level order traversal differ from the three depth-first traversals?
Why is a Queue used instead of recursion for level order?
Why must queue.size() be captured before the inner loop when grouping by level?
What data structure category does level order traversal belong to -- BFS or DFS?

sections.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its level order traversal as a list of lists (each inner list holds one level's values, left to right).
hints: Capture queue.size() before the inner loop to know exactly how many nodes belong to the current level.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Standard BFS with a queue, processing one full level at a time by snapshotting the queue size before draining it.

sections.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal II
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its level order traversal from leaf level up to root level (bottom-up).
hints: Do standard top-down level order, then reverse the outer list -- or insert each new level at the front.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Same BFS as normal level order, but insert each completed level at the FRONT of a LinkedList result so the final order is bottom-up.

sections.title: Binary Tree Right Side View
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the values visible from the right side, ordered top to bottom (one value per level -- the rightmost node at each level).

examples.explanation: Level 0: only 1. Level 1: 2 and 3, rightmost is 3. Level 2: 5 and 4, rightmost is 4.

hints: BFS level order, take the last node visited in each level.
Alternative: DFS(right first) and record the first node seen at each depth.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: BFS level by level; the last element processed in each level's inner loop is the rightmost node at that depth.

sections.title: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its level order traversal, alternating left-to-right then right-to-left for consecutive levels.

examples.explanation: Level 0 left-to-right: [3]. Level 1 reversed: [20,9]. Level 2 left-to-right: [15,7].

hints: Do normal BFS level order, then reverse alternate levels' lists (every other level, starting with the 2nd).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Standard BFS level order; track a boolean flag toggled each level, and reverse the level's list when the flag says right-to-left.

sections.title: Find Largest Value in Each Tree Row
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return an array of the largest value in each row (level).

examples.explanation: Level 0: max(1) = 1. Level 1: max(3,2) = 3. Level 2: max(5,3,9) = 9.

hints: BFS level order, track the max seen while draining each level.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: BFS level by level, tracking the running max within each level's inner loop.

sections.title: Maximum Level Sum of a Binary Tree
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree (levels numbered starting at 1), return the level number with the maximum sum of node values. If multiple levels tie, return the smallest level number.

examples.explanation: Level 1 sum = 1. Level 2 sum = 7 + 0 = 7. Level 3 sum = 7 + (-8) = -1. Level 2 has the max sum (7), so answer is 2.

hints: BFS level order, sum each level, track the best (level, sum) pair using strict '>' so ties keep the earliest level.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: BFS level by level, summing each level's values and updating the best level using strict greater-than so the first (smallest-numbered) level wins ties.
